title: Prevalence rate of hypertension in adults aged 30-79
subtitle: Estimated share of the population with [hypertension](#dod:hypertension), based on population representative surveys and statistical modeling. Hypertension is a [risk factor](#dod:risk-factor) for chronic complications, including [cardiovascular disease](#dod:cvd), and premature death.
note: To allow for comparisons between countries and over time, this metric is [age-standardized](#dod:age_standardized). The data is restricted to those aged 30–79 years because hypertension prevalence is low before age 30 years and because guidelines differ in thresholds and treatment targets in older age groups.
caption:
Prevalence rate of hypertension in adults aged 30-79

Estimated share of the population with hypertension, based on population representative surveys and statistical modeling. Hypertension is a risk factor for chronic complications, including cardiovascular disease, and premature death.

This choropleth world map displays the 2019 prevalence rate of hypertension in adults aged 30 to 79. The legend uses a blue color scale ranging from 30% to 45%, with specific tick marks at 30%, 35%, 40%, and 45%. The darkest blue shades, indicating prevalence rates at or above 45%, are visible in specific regions including Argentina, Uruguay, and parts of Eastern Europe such as Hungary and Slovakia. A broad cluster of countries in South America, including Brazil and Chile, along with nations in Central and Eastern Europe, appear in mid-range blue shades that fall between the 35% and 40% thresholds. In Africa, a band of countries across the Sahel and Southern Africa sits in the 35% to 40% range, while North African nations generally display lighter shades closer to the 30% mark. The United States, Canada, and Australia sit in the lighter end of the spectrum, appearing in pale blue shades near the 30% to 35% boundary. Greenland is marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no data. The metric is age-standardized to allow for comparisons between countries and over time, and the data is restricted to the 30–79 age group because hypertension prevalence is low before age 30 and guidelines differ for older populations. The visual distribution shows the highest prevalence rates concentrated in parts of South America and Eastern Europe, with lower rates observed across much of North America, Oceania, and parts of Africa.
